my_thoughts: 'I love her but I hate her'—they experience this as contradiction. How can both be true? Teacher's distinction resolves it: love IDEAL mother (who you wish existed) vs hate REAL mother (who actually abused you). Two different objects: wish vs reality. This removes guilt about 'hating mother' (you don't hate the ideal, you hate the abuse). Single-turn reframe gives them language for complex feeling. Not contradiction but two separate loves/hates.
accumulated_insight: T1 (Single-turn): Student states paradox about parent: 'I love her but I hate her.' Teacher reframes: 'You love the mother you wish you had, and you hate the mother you actually have.' Separates idealized (wished-for) mother from real (abusive) mother. This is RM_01: distinction that resolves paradox. Can love ideal AND hate reality without contradiction. Predicting surface_emotion (reframe typically produces recognition).
visible_response:
But whenever your love goes against your freedom, you are doing something else in the name of love.
What you call love I call self-hatred.
